app.name: Compass
app.defaultBubble: Hi there! I'm Compass, your Sheffield career planning guide. I'm here to help you explore who you are, what's out there, and how to find your direction. What are you working on today?

stages.label: What do I know about myself?
stages.bubble: Great place to start  Understanding yourself is the foundation of good career decisions. Let's look at your values, strengths, skills, and interests.

topics.label: My values and personality
topics.title: What do I know about myself?
topics.desc: Understanding your values and personality is where career planning begins — not with job titles, but with who you are.
topics.squig: 💡 There's no right answer here. The goal is insight, not certainty. Most people discover their values more clearly through experience than through reflection alone.
reflects: What matters most to you in a working life — people, creativity, impact, structure, flexibility?
When have you felt most like yourself? What were you doing?
What would you find genuinely difficult to tolerate in a job, day-to-day?

resources.name: Prospects Job Match
resources.desc: Answer questions to match your personality to real job roles

resources.name: Who You Are
resources.desc: Guided self-assessment: values, interests, personality

resources.name: Reflective CV + mySkills
resources.desc: Connect your experiences to skills with mySkills

resources.name: Sheffield Strengths
resources.desc: Discover and articulate your top strengths

myths.chip: "I should know what I want to do by now"
myths.bust: Most people don't — and that's completely normal. Research shows most graduates end up in roles they hadn't planned. Exploring is the work, not a sign you're behind.

myths.chip: "I don't have any real skills yet"
myths.bust: You have more than you think. Part-time work, sports, societies, caring responsibilities, creative projects — these all develop transferable skills employers value.

topics.label: My skills and strengths
topics.title: My skills and strengths
topics.desc: Skills are often invisible to us — we underestimate what we've built. This section helps you name them clearly.
topics.squig: 🌱 Skills you've developed outside your degree — in jobs, volunteering, or life — are just as valid to employers. Don't filter them out.
reflects: What do people come to you for help with? What do you get complimented on?
Which of your experiences stretched you the most? What did you learn?
What skills do you most want to develop in the next few years?

resources.name: mySkills
resources.desc: Map your experiences to skills and generate evidence statements

resources.name: Reflective CV
resources.desc: A CV format designed to surface skills, not just list jobs

resources.name: Skills Audit
resources.desc: Identify gaps and priorities for your development

resources.name: Book an Appointment
resources.desc: Talk through your skills profile with a careers adviser

myths.bust: Grade requirements vary hugely — many roles don't have them at all. Skills, attitude, resilience, and experience consistently outrank degree classification in employer surveys.
Entry-level roles expect you to be entry-level. Apply with what you have and be honest about what you're learning.

topics.label: Explore job matches
topics.title: Explore job matches
topics.desc: Sometimes the best way to understand yourself is to see what resonates — and what doesn't. Job matching tools are a starting point, not a verdict.
topics.squig: 🧩 Treat job match results as prompts for curiosity, not prescriptions. If something surprises you, that's useful information too.
reflects: Which results interest you — even ones you'd never considered before?
Which results feel completely wrong? What does that tell you about your priorities?
Is there a pattern across the roles that appeal to you?

resources.name: Prospects Job Match
resources.desc: Personality-based job matching — free, quick, and genuinely useful

resources.name: Career Quiz (Prospects)
resources.desc: Explore broad career areas based on your interests

resources.name: Prospects Job Profiles
resources.desc: In-depth guides to hundreds of job roles

resources.name: Book an Appointment
resources.desc: Explore what your results mean with a careers adviser

myths.chip: "If a job isn't in my results, it's not for me"
myths.bust: Job matching tools are a starting point, not a ceiling. Many people build fulfilling careers in areas their quiz never suggested.

topics.label: Squiggly careers
topics.title: Squiggly careers
topics.desc: The idea that you choose a career at 21 and follow it in a straight line is a myth. Most careers are squiggly — and that's actually a superpower.
topics.squig: ↗️ A squiggly career means changing direction, trying things, and bringing unexpected combinations of experience together. Employers increasingly value this.
reflects: Can you think of someone whose career path you admire, even though it wasn't straight?
What would you try if you knew it was okay to change direction later?
What does a successful career look like to you — really?

resources.name: Squiggly Careers
resources.desc: The book and community redefining what career success looks like

resources.name: Graduate Outcomes
resources.desc: See the actual squiggly paths Sheffield graduates have taken

resources.name: LinkedIn Alumni
resources.desc: Real career journeys from Sheffield graduates — rarely linear

resources.name: Careers Podcast
resources.desc: Squiggly career stories from people across every sector

myths.bust: Everything you've done comes with you. Skills, relationships, and experience transfer — often in ways you can't predict until you're there.

myths.chip: "I need a plan before I can do anything"
myths.bust: Action creates clarity more reliably than planning does. Sometimes you need to try something to know whether you want more of it.

stages.label: What do I know about jobs?
stages.bubble: Good thinking  Understanding the world of work — what roles exist, what's really involved, and what graduates actually do — changes how you see your options.

topics.label: What do graduates actually do?
topics.title: What do graduates actually do?
topics.desc: Graduate Outcomes data tracks what Sheffield graduates are doing 15 months after finishing their degree. The results often surprise people.
topics.squig: 📊 Most graduates aren't in the jobs you'd expect from their degree title — and that's the point. Labour market knowledge opens doors you didn't know existed.
reflects: Do you know what graduates from your course actually go on to do? Have you looked?
Are there paths you've assumed are closed to you that might not be?
What would it mean for your plans if you knew more people take unexpected routes?

resources.name: Graduate Outcomes
resources.desc: Sheffield-specific data on what graduates do 15 months after graduating

resources.name: LinkedIn Alumni
resources.desc: Real Sheffield graduates — search by sector, role, or company

resources.name: Prospects
resources.desc: Job profiles, sector guides, and typical progression routes

resources.name: Book an Appointment
resources.desc: Interpret what the data means for your situation

myths.bust: The majority of graduate jobs are open to any degree subject. Your degree is one signal among many — what you've done with your time matters just as much.

myths.chip: "Graduate schemes are the only graduate jobs"
myths.bust: Graduate schemes employ a tiny fraction of graduates. The vast majority of graduate-level work is recruited through direct applications, speculative approaches, and networks.

topics.label: Researching job roles and sectors
topics.title: Researching job roles and sectors
topics.desc: Most people pick careers based on limited information. The more you know about what roles actually involve — day-to-day — the better your decisions become.
topics.squig: 🔍 Read job adverts not to apply, but to learn. Notice what skills and experiences keep appearing across roles you find interesting.
reflects: Have you read job descriptions for roles you're drawn to? What surprised you?
Which sectors do you know almost nothing about that might be worth exploring?
What would a good working day look like to you in five years?

resources.name: Prospects Job Profiles
resources.desc: Detailed role guides covering duties, salary, entry routes, and progression

resources.name: Labour Market Info
resources.desc: Which sectors are growing, where the opportunities are

resources.name: LinkedIn
resources.desc: Research companies, roles, and people in areas that interest you

resources.name: Sector Guides
resources.desc: Careers Service guides to the major industries and sectors

myths.bust: Many skills transfer across sectors. Exploring two or three areas in parallel is a strategy, not indecision.

topics.label: What employers really want
topics.title: What employers really want
topics.desc: There's a gap between what students think employers want and what they actually look for. Understanding this changes how you present yourself.
topics.squig: 💡 Employers consistently say attitude, curiosity, and communication matter more than technical knowledge — especially at entry level.
reflects: What do you think employers in your area of interest are looking for? Have you checked?
Where in your experience can you evidence curiosity, resilience, or collaboration?
What do you know about the culture of organisations you're interested in?

resources.name: Graduate Attributes
resources.desc: What Sheffield graduates are known for — and how to evidence them

resources.name: Company Research
resources.desc: How to go beyond the website and understand what an employer is really like

resources.name: Career Mentoring
resources.desc: Talk to professionals in your target area about what they look for

resources.name: Book an Appointment
resources.desc: Get a read on how you're coming across to employers

myths.chip: "I need a 2:1 to get a good job"
myths.bust: Many employers have removed grade requirements entirely. Where they exist, a strong application and evidence of skills can outweigh a grade boundary.

myths.chip: "I'm not the type they're looking for"
myths.bust: Employers are actively trying to hire from diverse backgrounds. Your perspective, experience, and identity are things many employers are specifically seeking.

stages.label: How can I find what's right for me?
stages.bubble: This is where it gets real  The best way to find out what's right for you is to talk to people, try things, and test your thinking against reality.

topics.label: Talk to people in real jobs
topics.title: Talk to people in real jobs
topics.desc: Information interviews — short conversations with people doing work you're curious about — are one of the most effective and underused career tools there is.
topics.squig: 🗣️ Most professionals are happy to give 20 minutes to a student who asks thoughtfully. The worst they can say is no — and most don't.
reflects: Is there someone doing work you find interesting who you could reach out to this week?
What would you want to know about a job that you can't find on a website?
Who in your existing network works in areas you've never thought to ask about?

resources.name: LinkedIn Alumni
resources.desc: Find Sheffield graduates to reach out to for informational conversations

resources.name: Career Mentoring
resources.desc: Be matched with a professional in a sector you want to explore

resources.name: Work Shadowing
resources.desc: See a role in action — even a day can transform how you see a career

resources.name: Careers Events
resources.desc: Meet employers, alumni, and professionals at Sheffield events

myths.bust: A brief, genuine, well-researched message asking for a short conversation is almost universally welcomed. People remember asking for help early in their careers — they want to pay it forward.
Everyone starts with the same network: zero. LinkedIn alumni tools, careers events, and mentoring schemes exist precisely to give you a starting point.

topics.label: Try before you decide
topics.title: Try before you decide
topics.desc: You don't have to be certain before you take action. Work experience, short placements, and volunteering are ways of testing a direction before committing to it.
topics.squig: 🌱 Action creates clarity. Trying something and finding it's not for you is not a failure — it's data that makes your next decision better.
reflects: What experience would help you feel more confident about a direction?
Are there low-stakes ways to test an interest — volunteering, student projects, online courses?
What have you already done that's given you a feel for what kind of work suits you?

resources.name: Work Shadowing
resources.desc: Short placements arranged through the Careers Service

resources.name: Placements & Internships
resources.desc: Longer work experience opportunities within and outside your course

resources.name: Volunteering
resources.desc: Build skills and test interests through structured volunteering

resources.name: Sheffield Internships
resources.desc: Paid internships with Sheffield-based employers

myths.bust: The Careers Service offers funded internships, bursaries for unpaid work, and paid part-time opportunities. Ask before assuming you can't afford to get experience.

topics.label: Finding your ikigai
topics.title: Finding your ikigai
topics.desc: Ikigai is a Japanese concept for the place where what you love, what you're good at, what the world needs, and what you can be paid for all overlap. It's a useful lens, not a formula.
topics.squig: 🔮 Most people find their ikigai through years of living, not a single exercise. Use it as a prompt for reflection, not a destination to reach.
reflects: What do you do where time seems to disappear? What are you doing?
What problems in the world do you find yourself genuinely caring about?
Where do your skills and your values overlap — even partially?

resources.name: Ikigai Framework
resources.desc: A visual tool for exploring the four circles and their overlaps

resources.name: Values Reflection
resources.desc: Clarify what matters most to you as a starting point

resources.name: Ruthkrichards.co.uk
resources.desc: Practical Ikigai guidance for career decision-making

resources.name: Book an Appointment
resources.desc: Work through your ikigai with a careers adviser

myths.chip: "I need to find my passion before I start"
myths.bust: Passion often follows mastery, not the other way around. Many people find deep satisfaction in work they grew into, not work they felt certain about from day one.

myths.chip: "There's one perfect career out there for me"
myths.bust: There are likely many careers that could suit you well. The goal isn't to find the one right answer — it's to make a good enough decision and keep learning.

topics.label: Book careers support
topics.title: Book careers support
topics.desc: The Careers & Employability Service offers one-to-one support for any question at any stage — from 'I have no idea what to do' to 'I have an interview next week'.
topics.squig: 📅 You can access careers support for up to 5 years after graduating. There is no deadline for getting help — and no question is too basic.
reflects: What's the one thing you most want clarity on right now?
What's been stopping you from getting careers support sooner?
What would feel different if you had a clearer sense of direction?

resources.name: Book an Appointment
resources.desc: One-to-one appointments with a careers adviser — online or in person

resources.name: Drop-in Sessions
resources.desc: Quick questions answered without an appointment

resources.name: Career Mentoring
resources.desc: Be matched with a professional mentor in your area of interest

resources.name: Email the Careers Service
resources.desc: careers@sheffield.ac.uk — we're here to help

myths.chip: "Careers appointments are only for people who know what they want"
myths.bust: The most common reason people come is 'I have no idea what I want to do.' That's not just okay — it's where careers advisers are most useful.
Sheffield graduates can access full careers support for 5 years after graduating. You haven't missed the window.
